title: Case Study: NSECE Survey Data Analysis
text: vignette walks complete DER analysis nsece_demo dataset, synthetic dataset modeled 2019 National Survey Early Care Education (NSECE). end, able : Complete full DER workflow data exploration selective correction. Interpret design effects context early childhood policy research. Diagnose parameters design-sensitive explain using decomposition theorems. Apply selective correction compare naive blanket alternatives. Report DER results publication-ready format. analysis proceeds seven stages: explore survey design, run diagnostic, interpret fixed random effects, verify decomposition theorems conservation law, compare correction strategies, assess sensitivity threshold, format results publication.

title: Case Study: NSECE Survey Data Analysis
text: National Survey Early Care Education (NSECE), administered U.S. Department Health Human Services, primary data source understanding nation’s childcare landscape. 2019 wave surveyed childcare providers across 50 states plus District Columbia using complex multi-stage design: Stratification state ensures adequate representation jurisdiction. Cluster sampling within states (primary sampling units, PSUs) captures geographic correlation. Unequal selection probabilities oversample small states rare provider types. resulting survey weights can vary factor 10 , producing substantial Kish design effect. nsece_demo dataset synthetic analog preserves structural features.
code: data(nsece_demo)

title: Case Study: NSECE Survey Data Analysis
text: model examines binary outcome: whether childcare provider participates state’s Infant-Toddler () quality improvement system. Three fixed-effect covariates enter hierarchical logistic regression: β0\beta_0 (intercept): overall log-odds system participation. β1\beta_1 (poverty_cwc): provider-level poverty, centered within state. within-cluster covariate: varies across providers within state. β2\beta_2 (tiered_reim): binary indicator whether state operates tiered reimbursement policy. -cluster covariate: takes single value providers given state. addition, model includes 51 state-level random effects θ1,…,θ51\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{51}, total 54 parameters. DER analysis reveal 54 parameters sensitive survey design protected hierarchical structure.
code: nsece_demo$param_types #> [1] "fe_between" "fe_within"  "fe_between"

title: Case Study: NSECE Survey Data Analysis

# 95% credible intervals for the flagged parameter (column 2) ci_naive     <- quantile(draws_original[, 2],  probs = c(0.025, 0.975)) ci_corrected <- quantile(draws_corrected[, 2], probs = c(0.025, 0.975))  width_naive     <- diff(ci_naive) width_corrected <- diff(ci_corrected) width_ratio     <- width_corrected / width_naive  cat(sprintf("Naive CI:     [%.3f, %.3f]  (width = %.3f)
",             ci_naive[1], ci_naive[2], width_naive)) #> Naive CI:     [-0.201, -0.100]  (width = 0.101) cat(sprintf("Corrected CI: [%.3f, %.3f]  (width = %.3f)
",             ci_corrected[1], ci_corrected[2], width_corrected)) #> Corrected CI: [-0.234, -0.068]  (width = 0.166) cat(sprintf("Width ratio:  %.1f%%
", width_ratio * 100)) #> Width ratio:  163.9%

code: if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(ggplot2)    fe_names <- c("Intercept", "Poverty (CWC)", "Tiered Reim.")   ci_list  <- lapply(1:3, function(k) {     naive_ci <- quantile(draws_original[, k],  probs = c(0.025, 0.50, 0.975))     corr_ci  <- quantile(draws_corrected[, k], probs = c(0.025, 0.50, 0.975))     data.frame(       param  = fe_names[k],       type   = c("Naive", "Corrected"),       lo     = c(naive_ci[1], corr_ci[1]),       mid    = c(naive_ci[2], corr_ci[2]),       hi     = c(naive_ci[3], corr_ci[3]),       stringsAsFactors = FALSE     )   })   ci_df <- do.call(rbind, ci_list)   ci_df$param <- factor(ci_df$param, levels = rev(fe_names))   ci_df$type  <- factor(ci_df$type, levels = c("Naive", "Corrected"))    ggplot(ci_df, aes(x = mid, y = param, colour = type)) +     geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",                colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.5) +     geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi),                     position = position_dodge(width = 0.4),                     size = 0.5, linewidth = 0.7) +     scale_colour_manual(       values = c(Naive = "grey55", Corrected = pal["tier_ia"]),       name = "Estimate"     ) +     labs(x = "Coefficient value", y = NULL,          title = "Fixed Effect Credible Intervals: Naive vs. Corrected") +     theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +     theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),           legend.position = "bottom") }

what: The Problem: Why Do We Need DER?
title: Getting Started with svyder
text: Consider hierarchical logistic regression state-level random effects, fitted data complex survey. model three types parameters: yi∣β,θj[]∼Bernoulli(logit−1(β0+β1xi+β2zj+θj)),θj∼N(0,σθ2), y_i \mid \beta, \theta_{j[]} \sim \text{Bernoulli}\bigl(\text{logit}^{-1}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \beta_2 z_j + \theta_j)\bigr), \quad \theta_j \sim N(0, \sigma_\theta^2), xix_i varies within clusters (e.g., individual poverty) zjz_j varies clusters (e.g., state policy). survey design ignored, posterior within-cluster coefficient β1\beta_1 can substantially narrow survey weights clustering inflate variance model account . classical fix — sandwich variance estimator — tells us “right” variance every parameter simultaneously, blanket Cholesky correction rescales entire posterior match. problem blanket correction devastating: treats 54 parameters identically. typical NSECE-like model: 1 parameter (within-state poverty coefficient) genuinely needs correction — DER approximately 2.6. 53 parameters (2 -state fixed effects + 51 random effects) DER well 1.0 — posteriors already well-calibrated conservative. Blanket correction forces corrected covariance match sandwich matrix parameters. random effects, can shrink credible intervals little 4% original width, destroying variance gains hierarchical shrinkage motivated model first place. DER framework provides third option: selective correction. Identify one parameter needs help, correct , leave 53 untouched. dilemma blanket correction. 54-parameter model, 1 parameter (1.9%) needs design-based correction. Blanket correction inappropriately modifies remaining 53 parameters, selective correction targets design-sensitive parameter.

title: The Compute-Classify-Correct Pipeline
text: svyder package implements three-step diagnostic pipeline Bayesian hierarchical models fitted complex survey data. step addresses specific question: Compute: much parameter’s posterior variance differ design-consistent target? Classify: parameters exceed tolerance threshold need correction? Correct: adjust posterior draws flagged parameters? pipeline can executed single call der_diagnose() three modular steps can inspected customized individually. end vignette able : Execute full pipeline using --one step--step approaches. Understand step’s inputs, outputs, computational details. Perform sensitivity analysis across threshold values assess robustness classification. Compare DER across different clustering definitions using der_compare(). Extract use corrected posterior draws downstream analysis.

title: The Compute-Classify-Correct Pipeline
text: pipeline processes posterior draws hierarchical Bayesian model three sequential stages: step produces updated svyder object carries forward previous results. design allows inspection intermediate stage makes pipeline pipe-friendly.
code: +------------------+   Posterior draws --> | Step 1: COMPUTE  | --> DER values   Survey metadata     | der_compute()    |     Sandwich matrices                       +------------------+     Per-group diagnostics                               |                               v                       +------------------+   Threshold tau ----> | Step 2: CLASSIFY | --> Tier assignments                       | der_classify()   |     Flagged parameters                       +------------------+                               |                               v                       +------------------+                       | Step 3: CORRECT  | --> Corrected draws                       | der_correct()    |     Scale factors                       +------------------+

title: The Compute-Classify-Correct Pipeline
text: compute step computational core pipeline. constructs sandwich variance matrix 𝐕sand\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand}} respects survey design, computes MCMC posterior covariance 𝚺MCMC\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}, calculates per-parameter DER.

title: The Compute-Classify-Correct Pipeline
text: compute step performs following operations: Observed information: Computes Hessian 𝐇obs\mathbf{H}_{\text{obs}} weighted log-posterior posterior mean. Clustered score matrix: Aggregates individual score contributions PSU form Jcluster×dJ_{\text{cluster}} \times d score matrix 𝐉cluster\mathbf{J}_{\text{cluster}}. Sandwich variance: Forms 𝐕sand=𝐇obs−1𝐉cluster′𝐉cluster𝐇obs−1\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand}} = \mathbf{H}_{\text{obs}}^{-1} \, \mathbf{J}_{\text{cluster}}' \mathbf{J}_{\text{cluster}} \, \mathbf{H}_{\text{obs}}^{-1}. DER: Takes elementwise ratio diagonals: DERp=[𝐕sand]pp/[𝚺MCMC]pp\text{DER}_p = [\mathbf{V}_{\text{sand}}]_{pp} / [\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\text{MCMC}}]_{pp}. computational cost dominated matrix operations scales O(d2)O(d^2), d=p+Jd = p + J total number parameters.

title: The Compute-Classify-Correct Pipeline
text: classify step assigns parameter design-sensitivity tier flags whose DER exceeds threshold τ\tau.

title: The Compute-Classify-Correct Pipeline

text: Guidelines threshold selection: τ=1.0\tau = 1.0: Flag parameter DER 1. Conservative — corrects even small design effects. Appropriate exact coverage calibration paramount. τ=1.2\tau = 1.2 (default): posterior variance need increase least 20%. Balances sensitivity specificity. τ=1.5\tau = 1.5–2.02.0: large design effects trigger correction. Appropriate want preserve model-based inference correct egregious cases. sensitivity analysis helps verify substantive conclusions change across reasonable range thresholds.

text: comparison reveals choice clustering level affects DER diagnostics: Finer clustering (PSU level): Captures survey design’s correlation structure, typically producing larger DER values within-cluster covariates. conservative approach. Coarser clustering (state level): May underestimate design effect substantial within-state, -PSU correlation. Practical recommendation: Use finest available clustering level (PSU) primary analysis. state-level comparison serves diagnostic assess sensitivity.

what: Choosing the Right Threshold: Practical Guidance

title: Understanding Design Effect Ratios
text: Let us compute DER NSECE-like data (complex survey) balanced HLR data (equal weights) see three regimes: three DER regimes illustrated across two datasets. Top: NSECE-like survey data complex design (DEFF approx 3.5). within-cluster poverty coefficient (beta[2]) -dispersed (DER > 1), -cluster fixed effects random effects -dispersed (DER < 1). Bottom: balanced Gaussian HLR equal weights (DEFF = 1). parameters near 1.0, confirming design effects. contrast two panels illustrates DER’s discriminating power. NSECE data shows three regimes clearly separated, equal-weight data clusters entirely calibrated regime.
code: result_nsece <- der_diagnose(   x           = nsece_demo$draws,   y           = nsece_demo$y,   X           = nsece_demo$X,   group       = nsece_demo$group,   weights     = nsece_demo$weights,   psu         = nsece_demo$psu,   family      = nsece_demo$family,   sigma_theta = nsece_demo$sigma_theta,   param_types = nsece_demo$param_types,   tau         = 1.2 )  result_hlr <- der_diagnose(   x           = sim_hlr$draws,   y           = sim_hlr$y,   X           = sim_hlr$X,   group       = sim_hlr$group,   weights     = sim_hlr$weights,   psu         = sim_hlr$psu,   family      = sim_hlr$family,   sigma_theta = sim_hlr$sigma_theta,   sigma_e     = sim_hlr$sigma_e,   param_types = sim_hlr$param_types,   tau         = 1.2 ) if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(ggplot2)    td_nsece <- tidy.svyder(result_nsece)   td_hlr   <- tidy.svyder(result_hlr)    td_nsece$dataset <- "NSECE (complex survey, DEFF ~ 3.5)"   td_hlr$dataset   <- "Simulated HLR (equal weights, DEFF = 1)"    td_nsece$param_type <- result_nsece$classification$param_type   td_hlr$param_type   <- result_hlr$classification$param_type    # Assign regime labels   regime_label <- function(der) {     ifelse(der > 1.2, "Under-dispersed (DER > 1)",     ifelse(der > 0.8, "Calibrated (DER ~ 1)",                        "Over-dispersed (DER < 1)"))   }    td_nsece$regime <- regime_label(td_nsece$der)   td_hlr$regime   <- regime_label(td_hlr$der)    td_all <- rbind(td_nsece, td_hlr)   td_all$idx <- ave(seq_len(nrow(td_all)),                     td_all$dataset, FUN = seq_along)    ggplot(td_all, aes(x = as.numeric(idx), y = der, colour = regime)) +     geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "solid", colour = "grey70",                linewidth = 0.5) +     geom_hline(yintercept = 1.2, linetype = "dashed",                colour = pal["threshold"], linewidth = 0.6) +     geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 1.8) +     scale_colour_manual(       values = c("Under-dispersed (DER > 1)" = pal["tier_ia"],                  "Calibrated (DER ~ 1)"      = pal["tier_ib"],                  "Over-dispersed (DER < 1)"   = pal["tier_ii"]),       name = "Regime"     ) +     scale_y_log10() +     facet_wrap(~ dataset, ncol = 1, scales = "free_x") +     labs(x = "Parameter index", y = "DER (log scale)",          title = "DER Across Two Datasets: Three Regimes") +     theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +     theme(legend.position = "bottom",           panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),           strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11)) } #> Warning: No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual scale and the #> data's colour values. #> No shared levels found between `names(values)` of the manual scale and the #> data's colour values.

title: Understanding Design Effect Ratios
text: jjth random effect θj\theta_j: DERj=Bj×DEFFj×κj(J), \text{DER}_j = B_j \times \text{DEFF}_j \times \kappa_j(J), : Bj=σθ2/(σθ2+σe2/nj)B_j = \sigma_\theta^2 / (\sigma_\theta^2 + \sigma_e^2 / n_j) group-specific shrinkage factor. DEFFj\text{DEFF}_j group-specific design effect unequal weights within group jj. κj(J)=1−(1−Bj)/[J(1−Bj)+Bj]\kappa_j(J) = 1 - (1 - B_j) / [J(1 - B_j) + B_j] finite-group coupling factor (close 1 moderate JJ). crucial term BjB_j: shrinkage factor directly attenuates design effect reaches posterior. strong shrinkage (BjB_j small), random effect borrows heavily grand mean, leaving little room design effects distort posterior.

title: Compute Design Effect Ratios — der_compute
text: Computes Design Effect Ratios (DER) parameter Bayesian hierarchical model fitted complex survey data. DER measures much parameter's posterior variance change survey design properly accounted .

title: Compute Design Effect Ratios — der_compute
code: der_compute(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' der_compute(   x,   ...,   y,   X,   group,   weights,   psu = NULL,   family = "binomial",   sigma_theta,   sigma_e = NULL,   beta_prior_sd = 5,   param_types = NULL,   design = NULL )  # Default S3 method der_compute(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'brmsfit' der_compute(   x,   ...,   weights,   group = NULL,   psu = NULL,   family = NULL,   sigma_theta = NULL,   sigma_e = NULL,   beta_prior_sd = 5,   param_types = NULL,   design = NULL )  # S3 method for class 'CmdStanMCMC' der_compute(   x,   ...,   y,   X,   group,   weights,   psu = NULL,   family = "binomial",   sigma_theta,   sigma_e = NULL,   beta_prior_sd = 5,   param_types = NULL,   design = NULL )  # S3 method for class 'stanreg' der_compute(   x,   ...,   weights,   psu = NULL,   sigma_theta = NULL,   sigma_e = NULL,   beta_prior_sd = 5,   param_types = NULL,   design = NULL )

title: Compute Design Effect Ratios — der_compute
text: x draws matrix (S x d), brmsfit, CmdStanMCMC, stanreg object. matrix method, columns 1:p fixed effects columns (p+1):(p+J) random effects. ... Additional arguments passed methods. y Response vector (length N). X Design matrix (N x p). group Integer group indicator (1 J). weights Survey weights (positive, length N). psu PSU indicators (default: group). family Model family: "binomial" "gaussian". sigma_theta Estimated random effect SD. sigma_e Residual SD (gaussian ). beta_prior_sd Prior SD fixed effects (default: 5). param_types Character vector length p: "fe_within" "fe_between". design survey.design2 object (alternative weights/psu).

title: Compute Design Effect Ratios — der_compute
text: svyder object containing DER values, sandwich matrices, per-group diagnostics, original posterior draws.

title: Compute Design Effect Ratios — der_compute
text: DER defined ratio sandwich variance (accounts survey design) naive MCMC posterior variance. Values near 1 indicate posterior already well-calibrated; values substantially 1 indicate underestimation uncertainty. brmsfit method: brms method auto-detects response vector y, design matrix X, grouping variable group, model family, random effect SD sigma_theta fitted model object. user must provide weights (survey weights) optionally psu (primary sampling unit indicators), since stored brms fit objects. group argument can optionally provided override auto-detection random effects structure. CmdStanMCMC method: CmdStan store model data fit object, user must provide data arguments: y, X, group, weights, sigma_theta, optionally psu, sigma_e (gaussian), beta_prior_sd, param_types. draws extracted CmdStanMCMC object using extract_draws() passed matrix method. draws matrix must columns ordered [beta_1, ..., beta_p, theta_1, ..., theta_J]. stanreg method: rstanarm method auto-detects response vector y, design matrix X, grouping variable group, model family, random effect SD sigma_theta, residual SD sigma_e (gaussian) fitted model object. user must provide weights (survey weights) optionally psu (primary sampling unit indicators).

title: Compute Design Effect Ratios — der_compute

title: Decompose DER into Components — der_decompose
text: Decomposes parameter's DER constituent factors: Kish DEFF, shrinkage factor B, protection factor R_k, finite-J correction kappa. decomposition reveals parameter observed DER value.

code: der_decompose(x)

title: Decompose DER into Components — der_decompose
text: x svyder object.

title: Decompose DER into Components — der_decompose
text: data.frame columns: param, param_type, der, deff_mean, B_mean, R_k, kappa, der_predicted.

title: Decompose DER into Components — der_decompose
text: fixed effects: fe_within: DER \(\approx\) DEFF \(\cdot\) (1 - R_k), R_k \(\approx\) 0. fe_between: DER \(\approx\) DEFF \(\cdot\) (1 - R_k), R_k \(\approx\) B. random effects: DER \(\approx\) B \(\cdot\) DEFF \(\cdot\) kappa(J).

B_j Numeric vector shrinkage factors (length J). classification Data frame per-parameter classification results. tau Numeric scalar, flagging threshold DER. corrected_draws Numeric matrix, corrected posterior draws (M x d). scale_factors Numeric vector Cholesky scale factors (length d). original_draws Numeric matrix, original posterior draws (M x d). call matched call produced object. family Character string, model family (e.g., "binomial", "gaussian"). n_obs Integer, number observations. n_groups Integer, number groups/clusters. compute_time Numeric scalar, computation time seconds.

Contains N = 6785 observations across J = 51 states unequal survey weights, clustered PSU structure, three fixed-effect covariates (intercept, within-cluster poverty, -cluster tiered reimbursement policy).

what: svyder: Design Effect Ratio Diagnostics for Bayesian Survey Models — svyder-package
title: svyder: Design Effect Ratio Diagnostics for Bayesian Survey Models — svyder-package
text: Computes parameter-level Design Effect Ratios (DER) Bayesian hierarchical models fitted complex survey data. package implements compute-classify-correct diagnostic framework:

title: svyder: Design Effect Ratio Diagnostics for Bayesian Survey Models — svyder-package
text: Compute: Calculate DER parameter using sandwich variance estimator. Classify: Assign parameters three tiers based information source flag exceeding threshold. Correct: Apply selective Cholesky correction flagged parameters .

title: svyder: Design Effect Ratio Diagnostics for Bayesian Survey Models — svyder-package
text: der_compute(): Compute DER values. der_classify(): Three-tier classification. der_correct(): Selective Cholesky correction. der_diagnose(): --one wrapper.

title: svyder: Design Effect Ratio Diagnostics for Bayesian Survey Models — svyder-package
text: der_decompose(): Decompose DER DEFF, B, R_k, kappa. der_sensitivity(): Threshold sensitivity analysis. der_theorem_check(): Verify theoretical predictions. der_compare(): Compare across clustering definitions.

title: svyder: Design Effect Ratio Diagnostics for Bayesian Survey Models — svyder-package
text: brms: der_compute.brmsfit(), extract_draws.brmsfit() cmdstanr: der_compute.CmdStanMCMC(), extract_draws.CmdStanMCMC() rstanarm: der_compute.stanreg(), extract_draws.stanreg() survey: extract_design.survey.design2()

title: svyder: Design Effect Ratio Diagnostics for Bayesian Survey Models — svyder-package
text: nsece_demo: Synthetic NSECE-like survey data (binomial, J=51). sim_hlr: Balanced hierarchical linear regression (gaussian, J=10).

title: svyder: Design Effect Ratio Diagnostics for Bayesian Survey Models — svyder-package
text: Maintainer: JoonHo Lee jlee296@ua.edu (ORCID)

title: svyder 0.1.0
text: Initial release svyder package. Core pipeline: der_compute(), der_classify(), der_correct(), der_diagnose(). Analysis functions: der_decompose(), der_sensitivity(), der_theorem_check(), der_compare(). Backend support: brms, cmdstanr, rstanarm, survey package. Visualization: profile, decomposition, CI comparison plots. Tidy methods: tidy.svyder(), glance.svyder(). Two bundled datasets: nsece_demo sim_hlr.
